text: Ladies and gentlemen, this video is aimed at trainees and junior surgeons and will show a step-by-step approach to transanal advancement flap repair for perianal fistulas.
This popular type of repair was first described by Arthur Elting from Albany, New York, over a century ago.
Interestingly, the flap that was originally described by Elting is quite different from the flap many of us perform.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectal mucosa without making lateral incisions, advancing it caudally.
Even though few randomized data are available, several reviews have concluded that the weighted average healing rate of flap repair is about eighty percent.
Furthermore, it is the best investigated procedure for fistulas available, and should therefore be considered the gold standard for the treatment of complex perianal fistulas.
The present video will not cover patient selection and imaging.
If you are interested in these subjects visit the ESCP resource library and watch the video on patient selection and the video on MRI imaging of perianal fistulas.
Let me illustrate the steps of this procedure in a short animation.
A schematic drawing illustrates the external anal sphincter and internal sphincter, highlighting their anatomical positions.
The video shows the rectal mucosa, which appears smooth and pink, as part of the anal surgery procedure.
The video displays the dentate line, a distinct anatomical feature within the anal canal.
A complex trans-sphincteric fistula is visible, indicating the need for a complex repair technique such as transanal flap repair.
The surgeon begins the procedure by coring out the external part of the trans-fingleric fistula, using precise surgical techniques to excise the affected tissue.
The surgeon continues the operation intra-anally, excising the internal opening of the fistula, with visible tissue manipulation around the internal sphincter area.
The surgeon excises or curettes the remaining part of the trans-fingleric fistula, focusing on the internal anal region.
The surgeon completes the coring out of the entire fistula, resulting in a large visible defect in the tissue.
The surgeon creates a thick flap to close off the defect left by the excision of the fistula, preparing for complex repair.
The surgeon raises a thick flap over several centimeters to cover the defect, and then closes the internal opening of the fistula.
The surgeon advances the thick flap over the internal opening and secures it in place with sutures, covering the previously excised area.
In this way the defect is covered by healthy tissue and should close in eighty percent of cases.
Let's go over it step by step, beginning with step one, the core out fistulectomy.
The surgeon utilizes the Lone Star retractor to provide enhanced exposure for deeper tracts during the operation, allowing for better visualization and access to the surgical site.
It can sometimes be useful to leave the cetone in the tract, however, coring out over a probe is often impossible.
The surgeon uses a microcautery device, specifically a Colorado needle, to perform precise cauterization during the anal surgery procedure.
The surgeon carefully removes granulation tissue up to the external sphincter, ensuring the area is clear of any remaining tissue.
The surgeon displays the cored out fistula, which has been removed in one piece, showing the clean excision from the surrounding tissue.
There is some debate whether or not coring out is necessary.
A review by the Valencia Group found no difference between core-out and simple curettage.
There was however no control of retrospective source data.
Many surgeons would advocate core-out.
In some patients, in whom minimal separation is found, curettage can however be enough.
Remember that the aim is to remove all separation and then choose the best way to achieve this goal.
The surgeon performs an excision of the internal opening, carefully removing tissue from the anal region.
The surgeon ensures that the excision of the internal opening is sufficiently wide, carefully extending the incision to achieve adequate exposure.
The surgeon debrides all separated tissue, creating a large defect in the process, ensuring thorough removal of affected areas.
The surgeon ensures that the advancement flap is positioned to attach securely to healthy tissue at the distal part of the internal opening.
The surgeon excises a thin strip of healthy mucosa at the proximal end of the internal opening, ensuring the tip of the advancement flap is strong and healthy.
The surgeon temporarily packs the external wound with gauze to ensure hemostasis, controlling any visible bleeding at the site.
The surgeon removes the temporary packing from the external wound before proceeding to suture the internal opening.
The surgeon performs a large elliptical excision to remove all separated tissue, ensuring a healthy tip of the flap and creating a suitable landing zone for adequate closure.
A detailed view of the excised internal opening is shown, highlighting the precise removal of tissue.
The surgeon displays the de-epithelialized landing zone, which provides adequate coverage for the closed internal opening.
The surgeon begins the closure of the internal opening, preparing to suture the area after ensuring hemostasis and removing temporary packing.
The surgeon carefully closes the defect in the intra-sphincteric space, originating from the internal opening, using resorbable braided sutures.
The video shows a large defect in the tissue following adequate debridement, highlighting the extent of tissue removal necessary for the procedure.
The surgeon creates an advancement flap, preparing tissue to cover the previously closed internal opening.
The surgeon creates a thick advancement flap approximately five centimeters long, with a base that is visibly wider than its tip.
Many different types and thicknesses of flaps have been described.
Even though it is difficult to decide exactly what anatomical layers will be included in your flap,
There is quite some evidence available that thicker flaps perform better.
Please be aware that the impact on fecal continence will also be greater when more layers are included in the flap.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to create a surgical flap, a technique commonly employed in anal surgery.
We use the harmonic scalpel because it is quick and provides good hemostasis.
We emphasize that its use is not mandatory and can be relatively costly.
Eloquent hemostasis is mandatory in order to prevent hematoma under the flap.
There are some data available that the use of a Parkes retractor can be detrimental to functional outcome.
For this reason we use the Lone Star retractor, where necessary augmented with a disposable plastic retractor.
The surgeon creates a flap measuring four to five centimeters in length, ensuring tension-free coverage below the sutured internal opening.
The surgeon advances and fixates the flap over the surgical site, ensuring it is properly positioned for tension-free coverage.
The surgeon sutures the flap in place, utilizing one of several possible techniques to secure it over the surgical site.
The surgeon uses two barbed sutures to incrementally fixate the flap, starting from the proximal end and moving distally.
Other surgeons advocate first fixating the flap distally and then filling in the lateral sides with interrupted sutures.
Be aware that monofilament sutures in the anal canal can be quite bothersome.
Try to avoid this.
Be critical of the end result.
The surgeon ensures the flap is securely fixed and visually assesses its perfusion, indicating healthy blood flow within the tissue.
Usually good coverage, as shown in this picture, can be obtained using the described method.
Additional measures worth mentioning are that we operate only patients with anterior fistulas in prone position.
Lithotomy is often good enough.
We administer single-shot prophylactic antibiotics only.
Patients are admitted for one night.
We advise against bicycle riding for two to four months after the operation.
Finally, before you commence this operation, ask yourself these questions.
Is this operation the best option for your patient at this time?
Furthermore, are you the best to perform it?
Flap repair is a complicated procedure that is unsuitable for unexperienced hands.
It is imperative to have enough exposure to gain this experience
and you should ideally be aware of the results of your center.
I hope you will find this video useful for your daily practice.
If you have any questions or additional comments, please do not hesitate to contact me.
Thank you for your attention.
